The llrst speaker was Judgo I. W.
Ferris, nf Waxuhachie, the nominee of
Ellis county, by the IdUioaster conven
tion, for the position of delegute to the
Constitutional Convention. The Judge
commenced his address by referring to
a remark he had beard, to the ell'ect
that it wus not proper to sejd lawyers
to the Convention, ami contending
thai It was wrong; that the lawyers were
the proper persons to frame and make
a constitution, being better and more
fully acquainted with the principles,
needs and wants of a constitution
Xe favored a chuuge lu the Judiciary
of the State, by the adoption
of tho old County Court sys
tem, with Its chief justice, who
should have Jurisdiction lu civil cases
to the iinioiint of oue thousand dollars,
and in crluilnul cases to ull grades of
oll'enses below felony. He would take
the constitution of 1845 as the basis of
the now organic luw, With proper mod
lllcutloiis und changes to suit the
changed condition of the State. He
favored an uiury law, fixing the legal
rate of Interest at eight percent per
annum, with a limit of twelve per cent
ou special contract. Ho fuvored econ
omy in all departments of the govern
ment und a reductlou of salaries of all
ollleers to the lowest living rates;
yet be would not place
them at such ruinously low rates as
would debar bouest and competent
men from striving to secure them. Ho
fuvored bieuuial sessions of the legis
lature, and a reduction of per diem to
five dollars, with a fair compensation
for mileage. He favored the wiping
out of tho immigration bureau and the
office of assistant attorney general
was in favor of a thorough aud liberal
public school system. Upon educa
tion, he said, depended the future
greutness, happiness aud prosperity of
the people; without education, we
could do nothing. The Judge spoke at
considerable length upei this subject,
favoring a liberal and comprehensive
system that, while a blessing and ad
vantage to tho state, should, by tigiu
and equitable taxation, be made to
bear lightly on the people. He
was opposed to the people
being taxed by tbe State, county or
municipalities for the purpose of sub
sidizing auy system of iuterual im
provements. He favored a six years
term of olllce for judges, aud four years
for all other State and couuty officers,
aud concluded by .declaring bis oppo
sition to the scheme of ousting tho
present officials; tbey had been elected
by the people for a certain length of
time, and he, for oue, was not in favor
of depriving them of that to wbloU
tbey were justly entitled.
Judge Ferris was followed by Major
John Henry Brown, who, in a ten or
fifteen minutes address, reiterated his
former ideas aud views of what the
constitution should he.
Major Brown was followed by Col
onel John C. McCoy, who also briefly
reiterated his former views, replying,
in auswer to au interogatory, that be
favored the ousting of tbe present
State aud county officers, and desired
a new election ordered all around.

itely committed to the "stalwart'

The-Administration was of so little
acoount in politics thai this is of no

out influence in his party, is actually,

What form his interference with its

language when speaking on the sub-
jeot. Zach Chandler and Don Cam
eron were closeted with him tog two
hours the other day, and his conver
sion was announced immediately there
after.

claim is delusive and will have but a
short ruu as an iospirer of the rural
Radicals. It is true that tne Repub
licans carried the State iu what they
eall "sweeping manner, and that
Senator Conkling will doubtlesa be re
elected as tbe result. But it is not a

victory that, and nothing more and
oo Democrat will detract one iota
from the. honor that belongs to Mr.
Conklinc aa a party leader, however
radical and uodoinecratte be may be.
It is just as true that no Democrat
with judgment looks npon the result
there as in tbs least diabearteoiDg in
a party sense. Mr. Uonkiiog will oot
be a part of the iaue next year, dot
for home time to come, and they have
notfjing to fear against any other Radi
cal, for nobody except be. eould carry

Washington, April 9,1862.
Major -General MoClellan:
DlAR Si- Your dispatches com
plaining that yoa are not properly
sustained, while they do notofieod
do pain me very muchi Blenoker's
Division, was withdraws from you be
fore you left here, and job know, tb
pressure under which i did it, and, as
I thought, acquiesced in it certainly
not without reluctance Alter you
left I ascertained that less" than 5S0,-
000 unorganised men, without a single
field-battery, were all you designed to
be left for tbe defence of Manassas
J unetion, and part of this,' even, was
to go to General Hooker's old Division.
General Banks' corps, once designed
for Msaassaa Junction, was divided
and tied up on tbo lioe of th Win
chester ami Strasburg, and oould not
lesvo it without again exposing the
Upper Potomao and the Baltimore
and Ohio Railroad. This presented,
or would present, when MoDougall
and Sumner should' he gone, "a' great
temptation to the enemy," to. turn
back from the Rsppahannook, and
sack Washington. My explioit order
that Washington should, by the judg
ment of all the oommanders of the
army oorpe, be left entirely secure had
been neglected. It was precisely this
that drove me to detain MoDowell.
1 do not lorget that I, was satisfied
with your arrangement, to leave Banks
ManaBsas Junction, but when, fhet
arrangement was broken up, ana nom
ine; was substituted for it, of course I
wss not satisfied." I was constrained
to substitute something, for it myself,
Now! allow ine to ask, do you really
think I should permit the line from
Richmond via ManasBas Junction to
this oity to bo entirely ope p, except
what resistance could be presented by
less than 20,000 unorganised troops?
This is a quost'ioA whioh the country
will not allow me to evade.
There is a curious mystery about
the number of troopa now with you.
When I telegraphed you on the 6th
saying you had over 100,000 with you,
I had just obtained from the Secretary
ot War a statement taken, as be said,
from your own returns, making 108,'
000 then with you and en route to you.
You now say you will have but 85,-
000 when all en route to you shall
have reached you. How can this dis
crepancy of 23,000 be accounted for?
As to General Wool s oommand, 1 un
derstand it is doing for you preoisely
what b like number of your own would
have to do if that oommand was away
I suppose the whole foroe which has
gone forward for you is with you by
this time, and if so, I think it is the
preoise time for you to strike a blow.
By delay tbe enemy will relatively
gain upon you that is, he will gain
faster by fortifications sjid reinforce
ments than you can by reinforcements
atone. And once mere let me tell you,
it is indispensable to you that you
strike a blow. Iam powerless to help
this. You will do me the justice to
remember I always insisted that going
down the bay in search of a field, in
steud ol fighting at or near Manassas,
was only shifting and uot surmount
ing difficultythat you would find the
some enemy, and tbe same or equal
inlrencbments at either place.' The
country will not fail to note is now
nothing that the present hesitation
to move npon an intrenched enemy is
but th story of Manassas repeated,
I beg to aasur you that I have
never written you, or spoken to you in
greater kindness of feeling than now,
nor with a fuller purpoae to sustain
you so fares in my most anxious judg
ment I eonaitteotly can, bulyom must
act. Yours very truly,
A. Lincoln.

The intolerable draia upon popular in
dustry, flowiDg steadily anil imierceptib1y
till the tax-gatherer comes, and then con
verted into a torrent of imprecations, wm
dug by the present Constitution. The
mighty canal is denominated the judiciary
system. If lawyers interested in its main
tenance were not the sharpest people next
after editors in the world, courts, save for
the proper trial and punishment of crimi
nals, would soon cease to exist. They
cost infinitely more than they pay, and if
they had not been handed down to us as
heirlooms, invested with cobwtli3 of an
tiquity, the people, long ago, would have
voted the judiciary ystetr. an insuffer
able calamity. There are absolutely
thirty-five district judges in Texas,
each making law with marvelous rapidity
that makes the soberest barristers' heads
swim. These judges alone cost the people
$122,500 per annum. Two of theso- big
wigged gentlemen are holding courts in the
same county, each costing the plundered
people $7000 per annum, and each hns his
little satellite, in the shape of a noi.y dis
trict attorney, each costing $2400, and then,
to cap the climax of luxurious superfluities,
the Legislature, even now, ;lias created
about a half a' dozen more of theso delec
table courts, each costing $oo00. Hut these
little salaries, amounting, with those of
clerks and sheriffs and bailiffs, to not less
than $220,000 per annum, constitute only
a small part of sums really wasted
on civil courts and their adjuncts
Every case now requires the intervention of
a jury and while one jury is "setting" that
a verdict may be "hatched" there is an
other "nest-hiding'' while lawyers and court
arc in torture to find out what sort of an
egg will be laid and whether, if the verdict
do not "sound in damages" it may not be
both damaged and unsound; The ser
vices of these twenty-four jurors arc woith
to the State two dollars each per diem and
instead of paying this in products of iodus
try, they cost each this euui. In other
words there is surely a dead los9 of three
dollars daily to society for each juryman
while acquiring a thirst for office and idle
ness and whisky. The time of witnesses,
added to this of jurors, makes these inci
dents of a court, sitting ten days, cost more
than one thousand dollars. There are forty
of these costing forty thousand dollars each
term, or eighty thousand dollars, to be
added to the sum of judicial salaries. There
are certainly one thousand lawyers incident
to all these multitudinous courts, Federal
and State, in all the counties of the empire
of Texas. These arc the sharpest, if not
always the greatest, most intelligent and
conservative men of the community. They
were educated professionally becamo of
precocity of ono sort or another, moral or
intellectual. If their energies were devoted
to practical pursuits, agricultural or me
chanical, they would add annually to pub
lic wealth at least two thousand five
hundred dollars each; but they add noth
ing and abstract, each, this average sum
from the coffers of honest toil, and here we
have a dead loss to the State of five thou
sand dollars for each disciple of Coke, who,
whatever his virtues and genius, is a prac
tical "deadbeat" fattening on human fol
lies, and yet Solomon, in all his glory, was
not arrayed like one of these. The pro
fession costs the hard-handed producers of
the country one thousand times five thou
sand dollars or the weighty sum of five mil
lions per annum. Somebody must dig it
out of the ground. The lawyers do not,
and it doesn't grow on the trees. Suppose
we add together the items of the State's
account against the civil side of the courts.
The sum total of loss to society in dollars
and cent, to say nothing of popular idle
ness begotten by fascinations of collo
quial excellence expended upon honest
country bumpkins by the loquacious
profession, on the shady side of vil
lage green, is not less than five mil
lions per annum. When wo reflect
that courts and sheriffs and lawyers of Texas
do not collect, as we arc informed, and pay
over to plaintiffs half a million annually,
and that it costs the poor, unthinking peo
ple five millions to get tho hatf a million,
it seems wnco strange that the courts and
lawyers and judges are not incontinently
and indiscriminately knocked on the head.
It is simply a question of dollars aud cents,
of profit and loss. There is no profit, and
it is all dead loss. We have not mentioned
another little body of satellites that swing
in paler and narrower orbits about the re
splendent glories of the courts of record.
These lesser judicatories are especially use
ful in breeding rows among ignorant peo
ple. They have their little shysters and
constables to maintain a proper degree of
litigious animation in the lower strata of
society, and are especially .beneucent in
towns and cities in stirring up the negroes
and unlettered foreign populations. Of
thes there are perhaps five in each county,
costing each, .with their offices and wit
nesses and in idleness substituted for in
dustry, and lawyers and constables, $2000
per annum or a million and a halt dollars
absolutely wrested from tho products of
popular toil and used ia generating idleness
and vice. Railways enrich, courts and
idleness desolate and impoverish States,
and yet the peoplo and press embrace and
love and cherish and multiply the one ca
lamity and denounce and dread the surest
practical blessing that God's providence
ever vouchsafed to man. The more miles
of railway and fewer courts Texas pays for,
the richer the State, tke purer its morals,
the bradcr its intelligence and more rapid
its progress. So true is all thi,that many,
very sensibly, have long believed it were
wise to enact a prospective law declaring
that, after a fixed period, men bhall trade
upon honor Then honesty and an honest
fame would make credit inimitable, and a
stainless name, from childhood to th grave,
would hare limitless moneyed Talue.

cities depends upon the exclusion of the
non-freebolding rabble from the ballot-box.
Experience has demonstrated this absolute
necessity. It is well enough fr cowardly
pobticians in legislative bodies to prate
about "Reform" and "Economy," and talk
loadly about "reconstructing city and town
governments." The sport is worth the
candle snce the innocent people are duped
and the demagogue is still blest with their
unthinking approval. lie knows well
enough that no reform is effective, no change
productive of needful results, and that no
augmentation of the number of offices or of
salaries signifies anything as long as the
tenure of office depends upon purchasable
votes. As light follows day so the offi
cer is an epitome in virtue and interest and
blackguardism of those that invest him
with office. lie is no sooner elected than
he begins to use patronage and money to
assure a re-election, and the p&asion for
office grows with gratification and this
hell-born lust, as in Grant's case, would
spare neither public money, or the blood of
myriads of our race or an empire's wealth
or country's organic law in compassing the
aims of ignorant ambition. Each vulgar,
corrupt, selfish, besotted alderman or other
municipal officer born of a city's tan eul
lottc is only the impersonation of the vices
and interests of his electors. What signi
fies, to such people, the rate of taxation
They and their constituents squander or
steal the contents, but pay not a groat
into the city treasury. They even lack,
in most instances, that sympathy
which distinguishes unity of race,
and the governing is of one, the taxpay
ing of another, and wholly different race.
Since municipal legislation affects nothing
but property, and does not deal with civic
or personal rights, there is no reason in
fact or morals, save the moral cowardice of
demagogues, why the ballot-box, in munici
palities, towns, cities and counties, should
not be thoroughly expurgated. The New
York Keening 7W, knowing nothing prac
tically of the horrors of negro domination
in cities and recognizing only the one hun
dred and sixty millions of debt fastened
upon the city of New "York by the de
praved rabble, concludes an article on this
subject with the assertion that "if we de
prive the ignorant and unworthy and irca
pable of the right of suffrage in cities and
towns, we would soon be induced to go fur
ther and exclude them, even in political
election, from the ballot-box." In this
the Pud confesses that we would be taught
the value of restricted suffrage, and in this
confesses all we ask, and there remains only
the question of nerve to face the dema
gogue in New York and the negro and his
ncwly-choscn masters in cities and towns of
the South. The Post is driven at last, by
its own reflections, to the impotent concul-
sion, always reached by newspapers and
demagogues that-owc potency to-the rab
ble, and says
"Let the property owners, the substan
tial classes of society, interest themselves
actively and constantly in city affairs, and
the evils incident to the rule of municipal
corporations by majorities will be speedily
aoatea."
The non-freeholding, city ruling mob Is
inaccessible to decency. Its forces are
gathered and organized in whisky dens and
hash houses and in filthy caverns, into
which light and cleanliness and decency
never enter. Nothing but money or prom
ises of money or whisky can be used effec
tively, and even the Post hesitates, in the
face of these plain facts, to repeat the
absurd injunction. It is easy enough for
the editor to advise, but most difficult to
do. "We would be pleased to see William
Cullcn JJryant knocking down a voter on
the right, and thrusting a tfneup ef whisky
in the face of another on the left, after the
felicitous manner of the redoubtable Con
gressman, John Morrisscy. Or would the
iW buy forced certificates of registration,
and import black laborers from cotton
fields and railways to prevent the election
of thieves by the same agencies and vile
practices employed in their elevation. to
office? No, no! While the black rabble
votes, and especially where its superiority
in numbers is confessed, decency stands
aloof, even as the Port admits, and the in
telligent people of New York are repelled
from voting places by disgusting incidents
of the hour, and by more deplorable results
of elections. New York City, croaninjr
under evils originating in no other source
than this, finds its party leaders constantly
discovering the origin of calamities in some
provision of the city charter, and this is
constantly amended, and the Legislature, at
Albany, devotes half of each session, at the
instigation of 6harp fellows who would de
lude the innocent people, to reforming the
city's organic law. They laugh in their
sleeves, and wink at one another, while
duped taxpayers applaud. None know
!etter than these representatives of the
rabble and creatures of "universal suf
frage'' which means necessarily the dis
franchisement of intelligence and decency
that no reform, however many changes are
effected, is possible until the very fountain
itself tho ballot-box whence all, woes
flow, is purified.

AISTIN AND PltSLIC AND PRI
VATE BATH 9,

Texans begin to appreciate the value of
cold water. Lecturers on temperance nat
urally lapse into disquisitions involving the
virtues of baths, and in hot days of sultry
summer time the advantages and comfort of
frequent ablutions are more and more keen
ly appreciated. The discussion of plans of
irrigation in tho Legislature, as conceived
by Major Johns and others, begat, it is ber
lieved, a prejudice in that body against
whisky, and cold water in vast quantities,
in the eyes of Texan lawgivers, doubtless
has greater value to-day than ever before.
Every civilized person who now builds a
residence provides for baths, not as a lux
ury, but as a necessity. Health, comfort
and decency concur in popularizing old
Roman habits of personal cleanliness, in
dispensable to decency in these low lati
tudes. Recently this subject has been
widely discussed, and one Dr. Lyon Play
fair, a great European scholar, made a
speech on the subject in Edinburgh, and
asserted that bathing was a religious duty.
This being true, we earnestly hope that
preachers of Austin will not fail to imbue
the minds of their hearers with proper con
ceptions of the value of that virtue, per
fect personal cleanliness, which the Bible
often commends. Dr. Flayfair declares, as
we cannot admit, that the practice of bath
ing came into existence with the Reforma
tion. It is proverbially said that cleanli
ness ia next to godliness, and we suspect
that this rather flippant proverb is the only
basis of Dr. PJayfair's ai scrtion. Being a
Protestant, he of course regards the Refor
mation as a revival of godliness, and hence
announces that a revival of cleanliness must
have accompanied it. But it is well known
that the ancient Greeks and Romans were
addicted, to an intemperate extent, to bath
ing, and, indeed, consumed a large part of
their time at the public baths. It is possi
ble that after the capture of Constantinople
bathing became one of the lost arts in Eu
rope whence the term "dark ages" but
the evidence that such was the case is want
ing. Dr. Playfair's remark would hardly
need comment were it not that it gave
offense to zealous Catholics, who understood
him to mean that cleanliness is an exclu
sively Protestant virtue. It is not quite
certain that he did not have some such idea,
since there is a widespread conviction in
the Brit'sh mind that to Englishmen alone
have been revealed the pleasures and bene
fits of the bath tub.

Aside from the cost of armies and in
teresf account, the revenues required by
Grant's and the Czar's empires are nearly
the same. But the income of Russia, last
year, was in. excess of expenditures by
over $13,000,000, and tho total amount ex
pended was $313,030,233, while the cor
responding expenditures of our government
for the fiscal year of 1874 footed up to
$283,738,800, less than the total of Russia
about $30,000,000. But in order to make a
fairer comparison, after deducting the in
terest on the public debt, which is less than
we have to pay, and the cost of the army,
which is more than ours, the remaining ex
penditures .are $131,312,701 in Russia and
$130,303,038 in the United States; or, after
deducting the interest and army expenses
from the budgets of both countries, the
United States spends annually $3,000,000
more in administering the government than
does the great empire of Russia with its
80,000,000 of people, its expensive imperial
household, its distant outposts, and the
whole paraphernalia of protective equip
ment and national splendor which character
ize the reicn of the Czar. Peter the
Great had a happy faculty of dispos
ing of dishonest stewards, and govern
ment officials since his time have stood
somewhat in awe of the discipline which he
bequeathed. The point that a Russian
minister of finance first aims at is in the
reduction of expenses or the production of
greater efficiency with the same outlay, and
be would not find it healthy to experiment
upon the amount of burden that could be
imposed without breaking the backs of his
Majesty's subjects. On the contrary, only
a few days ago, when Congress discovered
deficit of some $40,000,000, instead of
lessening government expenditures, new
taxes were levied and no restriction was
dared to be placed upon the extravagance of
countless placemen. It costs more to col
lect than expend taxes, and while the peo
ple only hear of about $300,000,000 annu
ally accounted for, it costs one dollar to get
another into the Treasury.

Now that the great railway is to be built,
the people of Austin will naturally address
themselves to the rapid improvement of the
city in all its incidents of beauty and at
tractiveness. There is no tuph site in
America as this for schools and colleges,
and there is no countiy so rich in re
sources lor their maintenance. On theso
lofty heights about Austin where States'
intellect and genius are often gath.
ercd, there should be the choicest in
stitutions of learning and taste and
art. Therefore the profound interest
evinced by cultured people of the city in
the fortunes of the Library Association and
therefore the assurances given the accom
plished instructor of females, Col. W. W.
Fontaine, that he could secure the means
here of erecting a magnificent structure, as
ornamental as useful to Austin, to be devo
ted to the training of the duughtersof the
city and State. Nothing would do more to
make the State capital attractive, to
add value to property, to make our
homes delightful by gathering here
the beauty and taste and intelligence of
Texas, and nothing would be more pro
motive of this result than the achievement
of purposes contemplated by Col. Fon
tainp. He holds-lhe btiongcot possible tes
timonials of personal worth, of fitness for
taks he would assume, and we can only
commend him to the good people of the
capital.
STUPENDOUS ROBIIEHV.
It is the richest practical joke and
grande&t achievement of bold knavery of
our time by which Jay Gould, Fisk's pal
in magnificent villainy, has used the inno
cent Congress and pressot the country to
destroy the value of Pacific Mail Steam
ship Company stock. His ends are
achieved, the country was humbugged,
the stock destroyed in market value, and
Gould, having bought it, controls the line
from San Francisco to Canton, and now,
controlling the railroad from. Qm.ha to Sau
Francisco, proposes to girdle the globe
His power is such that Vanderbilt, of the
New York Central, or Torn Scott, of the
Pennsylvania Central or Canadian railway
lines, must be subordinated to his purposes,
He can make his own terms. Isn't it a pity
that the International is not finished to San
Bias? Then Gould would find all his pretty
schemes thwarted even by the genius of
Governor Coke and Mr. Grow. A rival
route and shorter route, never interrupted
by snow storms, would offer such facilities
for the world's commerce between the Orient
and New York, and that Gould's knavery
would be more effectively punished than if
he had his deserts and a felon's cell beside
Tweed, the modest robber who only levied
exactions, through the intervention of the
base rabble, upon a-single citv, while
Gould robs nn empire.
It will be observed that under the opera
tion of the civil rights law, as judicially in
tcrpreted down at Columbus, a negro has
no right to get shaved. The civil rights
bill, as shaped by Congress, does not apply
to barber shops, and as whacked between
the eyes by Judge Ireland, has no applica
bility to Texas hotels. The surest way now
known for a colored gentleman to have the
thickness of his skull and thinness of shins
tested is to poke his woolly crown into a
barber shop. One tried it, as stated, in the
nice little town of Columbus. He was
thrust out and kicked and beaten by his
fellow-countrymen from Africa, and then
fined twelve dollars and costs by a pious
magistrate of the peaceful village. Social
rights, as sought to be attained by law, are
impossible of acquisition. Each applica
tion of compulsory forco to induce the
blending of races only repels them the more
violently froni one another. Prejudices are
constantly aroused, that might sleep pro
foundly if let alone. And if the object be
to lead the way to misccgaention by leading
blacks and whites into the same abodes,
God enters His protest when the products
of unnatural alliances never survive beyond
three generations.
Howevkb stagnant society and govern
ment and industry may be elsewhere, it is
probable that a greater number of miles of
railway will be built next year in Texas
than in all American States. The Missouri,
Kansas and Texas Railway, 7S0 miles long,
stops nowhere and has no Southern connec
tions. By means of Governor Coke s ad
ministrative ability, difficulties have been
obviated, -conflicting interests reconciled,
and this great highway comes down iroaa
licago and Kansas City to Austin and
Laredo en route to tho Pacific; the Red
River and Rio Grande road makes its way
to EAgle Pass; the Mexican Congress in
vites these peaceful agencies of thorough
revolution from abroad; and two mighty
empires, that within the memory of living
men were warring against one another in a
terrible and deadly struggle, will soon stand
side by side, both free, both rich and pros
perous, and each shaping for itself, in con
formity with instincts of diverse race, the
highest possible degree of civilization.

Tlie Constitution is practically annulk-il
D4 the Federal governuieut ia ooly oryan
izei misrule. Individual frtedutn, like
tlmt of S ate, ia habitually violnted bd1
Federal, State and local indebtedness has
been created till taxation ia intolerable,
property valueless, and industry uaeless,
and thus ullenefs, crime and robbery and
outrige atalk abroud everywhere. Public
burdens are imposed, not to maintain
government and law and order, but to
foster apecial organ i z -d interests, anil labor
ia robbed of its rewards by a depreciated
curisucy. So terriole and streujj have
these calamities become that con upt'mn is
universal, and the very durability of our
ouce admirable federal t-y.tem becomes iues
tiouable. Reform or revolution are the
issues present" d by everj public fact of the
paat ten years. Kiirli and poor alike must
combine autinwt the robbers or money and
nuuiljers achieving a triumph in the com
ing presidential eiectiou, our whole syteui
and philosophy of government must be
transfoimed. While bowing befire the
majesty of the negro's virtues and woith
the yoke of despotism has been fastened
upon our own necks and "in freeing the
slave America is enslaved." We are soou
to choose a President, and in designating
thechxice of Texas the representative body
now defining a code of laws, under a new
government, for this commnweulth, may
exert a strong and bentliceut influence.
The convention at St. Louis sits at an
early day and the Democratic party, a
represented here at the capital, should
assert its purposes aud aspirations aud
designate its preferred candidate. We
should declare for a president who has
illustrated in public an 1 private life those
virtues that will as-ure the speedy- extirpa
tion of those p cubar vices and evil, that
make the feiteral system abboiretit to
honesty aud ruinous to industry. The de
mand for the hniiest administration of
public aifairs and for obedience to the plain
edicts of consn'utionill law renders the
currency question comparatively- insignifi
cant and the; moral and intellectual forces
and virtues of men must be the strong
elements of fact and character to
shape the preference of the Democracy of
Texas. Samuel J. Tilden, of New York,
has been tried everywhere. He has been
found wanting in nothing. He recognises
his oath of otlice. lie overturned Tammany,
sent Tweed into exile and consigned his
pals, with the canal thieves of the Empire
State, to the peniteutiaiy. He turns neither
to the right nor left, despising alike the
many and the few, when they obstruct the
course of honesty and reform. Demanding
obedience to law at the hands of others,
he concedes ooedience iu all his. public acts
to the country's law aud exercises no
doubtful powers. He is to day t'le very
impersonation of Amciicun hatred of wrong
ami rubbery aud nepotism uvt vulgar snob
ocrncy. He has lessened pu'ttic burdens as
was never done before by any governor of
Nj. Voik, aud-byhls pleudid virtues,
illustrated in his viori tin and just admin
istration of the goveruuieul ol the greatest
Sia'e, he has shown his finicss to shupe the
destinies of. the graudcsl confederation of
Scates, and Sumuel J. Tilden should be the
next President. That his ticket may boast
of incomparable intellectual and moral
strength and draw to its support the vast
multitude of people who reck little of
parties if proper men lead them, it occurs
to us that ex-Senator Trumbull, the ablest
iawyei of the Wes, the defender of An
drew Johnson when impeached, and fore
most juiist that ever presided over the
Judiciary Committee of the United States
S nnte, should be the Democratic nominee
for the Vice Presidency. The South will
surely vote the Democratic tick'et; we
wou'd win the West, and Trumbull draws
Schuiz after him, and the German aud
Conservative Republican vote of the West.
Tne ticket should be Tilden and Trumbull.
Tiieiie are now no newspaper or foreign
deadbeats preying upon the capital and
upon the innocent literary glow worms of
Austin, and, therefore, we may properly
inveigh against the vices of these intellec
tual "tramps." They come well dressed,
save one whose person aud morals alike
needed plunge baths, 'and it is cheaper to
pay them, each a dollar or two, than in
cur the possibility of wounding the sensi
bilities of unfortunate respectability. Very
certainly the donors called "subscribers"
legard this class of drummers s they do
mendicants, and as an evidencb of this fact
the most absurd aud worthless possible
books and newspapers and periodicals are
hawked about for sale or for subscription.
One sharper was selling Lot many
months g as a novelty, a book
issued from the press fifteen years ago, and
the fun f the juke was that the fellow
sold the volume for one dollar more than
its retail price in any bookstore. On the
flyleaf was tha fresh itnptintof "18715,"
prefixed to a book printed before the
Xoachian deluge of blood and villainy swept
over the litnd. It was not even a new edi
tion of an old book, and yet tlte good peo
ple bit and were bitten. Several volumes
of these books undelivered, for want of
stamps to pay cost of transportation, are
now stored in a neighboring city.
A lame man was traversing the streets in
all directions, not many months ag, secur
ing subscriptions to the Lot Va-iW," a
namby-pamby monthly published, we be
lieve, in N.irth Carolina, s The "Loat
Pleiad n pretended to be devoted to Texas
interests, the reason for which it was diffi
cult to hammer into the thick skull of msny
people; but then, it only cost fifty cents
per annum to find out why a literary ven
ture, tailing in North Carolina, should ap
peal to Texas for support save on the score
ot supposed stnpidity. But good-natured
lawgivers and simple-minded, innocent citi
zens invested in the trifle rather than offend
or refuse to serve the helpless drummer.
Foraiga pamphlets, Weckliea and books
printed t defraud the unlettered and rap-
plant, by persistent boring of lightning-rod
agent, the product of tbe Texas prt ss.
have ran their course, and idleneaa and
mifcfortooa aaonkl prey for tint, pon
other than the printer and publisher and
people of Texas.

Tilden seems still to lead the list of Dem
ocratic candidates, and the chances, just
now, evidently favor bis nomination. Til
den hus the tremendous advantage of being
sustained by the whole Conservative and
Democratic press of New York City. There
is no means of measuring its all pervading
power. The commercial metropolis of the
country, the focus of American intelligence,
the depository of its gold and the central
seat of newspaper power can hardly be re
sisted by any representative body gathered
even in St. Louis. Omnipotent and ubiqui
tous New York newspapers shape the action
of the party even before its managers ant
convoked in the new "Hnb," and each del
egate, going to the big fridge, crosses it
with the conviction that Tilden is the
strongest of all aspirants, and each repre
sentative of a district would have it said
that he foresaw results, and, from the be
ginning, stood by the winning candidate.
Especially is this true when each delegate
goes to St. Louis with the hope that some
thing will come of glory or honor or office
to himself from his action in this conven
tion. He would have some benefaction at
the hand of power, and therefore would
clasp it tightly, even before it wields
the scepter of presidential omnipotence!
It thus happens that all the time-serving',
timid members of the convention will
speedily rally about the banner of the hero
whose praises are most loud y chanted by
the ubiquitous newspapers of the East.
There is a fact of this, sort most unfor
tunate for our party. Not only does Pres
ideut Grant wield the consolidated power
of 03,000 placeholders, and of their kindred
and satellites, but government patronage
has been used to create newspapers and en
rich and gather intellectual resources and
give potency to the Republican press un
til its aggregate power is superior to that
which Tilden, if nominated, may wield.
Every United States marshal and the post
masters and placemen of his district is re
quired to maintain a well-oiled party organ.
Sharp, shrewd appointees of Radical place
holders turn the crank and there are no po
litical melodies chanted in Northern States
that do re echo the story of Southern mis
deeds, crimes and enormities. At this
hour there are countless myriads of listen
ers who believe that wails of murdered Af
ricans burden the very air breathed by the
people of Texas. While, therefore, the pow
erful press of New York will probably
nominate the candidate of the Democratic
patty, it is iqually true tlmt the great
danger of defeat incurred by Democracy
consists in the extraordinary force aud
power given Republican newspapers by the
Federal Treasury. Compared with organs
of Republican faith, those of the Democratic
party are less numerous everywhere iu the
North and they are unable to employ the
agencies of popularizing themselves to
which the President's exponent may resort.
Offices themselves consolidate a mighty
army about the President, and this terrible
organization exerts despotic sway through
the press it euriches over the opinions and
passions of the masses of Northern people.
It follow;, whatever may be said to the
contrary, that if the New York press nomi
nate, it must also elect the next President
or confess defeat at the hands of Tribunes,
Gozcttes and Inter-Oceans and Glube-Dono-erats.
THE GALVESTON BUKAK WATER.
We have written of the scheme of thoee
excellent gentlemen who would serve Gal
yebton so unselfishly with a share of seri
ousness becoming the peculiar propositions
presented before the Legislature. Break
water building involves not a little knowl
edge of hydrodynamics and of civil engin
eering, in which this proposed scheme is a
problem of the first magnitude. The prop
er definition of plans involves the employ
ment of educated skill and profound tech
nical learning. But if the proposed task
be feasible it involves an expenditure so
vast that we may well inquire, will the end
justify the means? If we invest a million,
will not the unfinished, imperfect task de
mand a million nore? When a city induces
Congress to found a public structure in
its midst the sum-tofal of appropriations,
when the structure is completed, is four
fold greater than the "original estimates."
Even more uncertain in cost, to assure per
fection, is the scheme defiued for the ap
proval of the lawgivers of Texas in behalf
of the artificial seawall designed to make
property absolutely secure that cost less be
cause insecure. People investing in Gal
veston paid prii.es defined by dan
gers from the encroaching sea, and
the public owes individuals nothing for
past or possible losses incurred, because
there is no breakwater to oppose the prog
ress of maddened billows. But conceding
the detiniteness ot sums demanded to ac
complish the end aimed at, the question
still arises whether Texas or the 'United
States government should assent to the de
mands of Galveston. A great number of
crude plans for the construction of this
break wter have been snbmitted, by the
learned and unlearned, to unskilled alder
men and legislators, but there has been ap
pointed no board of educated engineers to
tell the people and law-makers whether any
of these plans and specifications wilt "hold
water," or to advise us, even with proxi
mate accuracy, of the cost of executing on
land or water the pretty devices presented
on paper. We believe that the city fathers
of Galveston proposed to pay two hundred
and fifty dollars for the best plans aud
specifications, and very many learned aud
unlearned draughtsmen have sent forward
admirable picture of the sea coast; but of
the cost of the work, when finished, there
is no means of determining.

It is wholly impossible tor ingenious wri
ters of centennial show and Washington
City patent inside and outside letters to im
pose advertisements, in the shape of 'cor
respondence," upon sensibly-conducted
newspapers. Gratuitous advertising of all
sorts is finally cone for. It is wholly need
less to talk to a sane publisher about "the
p.iblic interest," "philanthropy," "piety"
or patriotism." We must live, and when
we give to the public or to public charity,
or to the church, we will give like other

sums than oilier citizens. Newspapers
should do no free advertising of any kind,
but demand a fair compensation for their

tion i- coutriliu' ions to benevolent a.nd other

tous ad vertisemeuts and have no paid mat

no departure.

iiii I have the honor to transmit here
with, for the consideration ot the Legisla

preme Court, showin the operations of

The fuels stated in this report are com
mended to the Legislature as of the first

port that 6iuce February 1, 1874, the date

six weeks time remains to complete the

year, and assuming that seventy-nine cases
wiil be disposed of, there will rem tin sev
en huudred cases undecided. More, than
half of these cases are stated by the Chief
Justice to involve or pertain to the title to
land, and are the most difficult cases before
the court, many of them having been passed
because of the difficulty of arriving at sat
isfactory conclusions. Taking into consid
eration the character of the cases, it is be
lieved, if five judges were on the bench in

en hundred cases would require two

sary. It is also believed that a

of the other two it practical administra

zen of the S.ate as does the judicial de
partment. BjIicviuk that the facts so clearly set

ter into the estimate in your deliberation

aa tbe word wa given, and coming off -him-elf

not unbecoming or distasteful in a man of

nirizd by the same correspondent. Gover
nor Wise's views are certainly entitled to

that be was neither a Conservative, nor

Democrats, old-fashioned Whigs, Know-"

WIIEELKR AND TILDK1, M THE
wntS OF TiKW VOHK.
VV heeler nonunatioa u candidate for
the Tioe-preaidency waa evidently designed
to destroy Tilden's 8npremacy in New
York. If Wheeler had Hayeg'g pi ace as
the Repnbl tcan leadey, .Tilden?a power in
l&e Empire State might be neutralized, bnt
the Republican ticket, u Jwe stated Satur
day, it strongest in its haunches, and the
vice-presidency ia the fatal paralysis
greatness. Wheeler's friends and admirers
in New York may be numberless, Vmt tlient
approving none the less the d.eeds and pur
poses of Tilden, would elevate the latter to
an office in which his peculiar virtues aud
opinions may be injected into the conduct
of the Federal government.' The elections
of presidents and vice-presidents become,
in the North, a mutter of busiunss, an? not,
as ia former years, ol "hard cider," or
hasty plates of soup," or squatter sove
reignty" br negro-worshipping demagog
ism. Great commercial States and cities
have profounder interests involved when
taxation is ruinous, and every dollar that
drifts along the channels of trade is elo
quent of hard times. Mr. Wheeler's mis
fortune, and that of his party, consists in
the fact, that if elected Vice-President, no
force of genius or statecraft can make his
potency felt in the country's legislation
New York, on the contrary, has tested Til-
uen's virtues, and New York, more than
nil the States, that prosperity and business
activity may be reproduced, will Burely ad
here to the fortunes of Tilden, an 3, for the
reason that if elected, he will make New
York great by aggrandizing the Union. The
Cincinnati convention confessed this fact by
nominating Wheeler in- order to break, if
possible, the force of Tilden's certain nom
ination. The. action of the convention at
Pincionati was thoa based on the assump
tion that Tilden would lead the Democratic
party, and Hayes and Wheeler were set up
as' barriers to the tide of public opinion,
demanding honesty and radical reform.
But i the stone' wall of bard money thus
lifted up will be overwhelmed. There are
broad and deep fissures in it which Hayes
and Wheeler cannot close. Reform is simply
impossible without a thorough political and
partisan revolution. Fixed diseases of ex
travagance and official corruption, gener
ated, through, fifteen years of unbroken
power,' cannot be cured by sedatives. He
roic treatment of the sorely ulcerated body
politic is a confessed necessity among the
toiliDg people and taxpayers, and Tilden
leading the Democratic party, and Wheeler
cipher in front of Radicalism, the result
in New York cannot be doubted. Wheeler
is therefore nominated to head off
Tilden, and thus Tilden's power is
confessed by Jti enemies. L The only
mistake made at Cincinnati was: af-
;er admitting Tildin's potency, that
Wheeler was not so used that the people
would be forced to confess that the triumph
of Wheeler's party would be the equivalent
of Tilden's investiture with the presidency.
The coming contest is one involving dol-
Urs and cents, aud Wheeler is impotent
and Hives devoid of that extraordinary
i
ntellectural and- moral vigor that distin
guishes the iron-handed chief magistrate
and reformer of New York.
ma. alhson's policy.
Mr. Allison, of the-House, tells his com
mittee that desperate diseases require dos-
perate- remedies, and that: horse or cattle
thieves must be banged."'- The people con-
uc wirb.. the venerabre lawgiver. They
demonstrate the .'.necessity for vigorous
measures by their own vigorous action.
The evil may be ephemeral, but none the
less .terrible1 and, .when remedied, .the
bloody code of to-day may be remodeled.
Every .newspaper, ou the continent tolls of
lawlessness 'prevalent in wide districts of
Texas. Whatever we may say to the con
trary, the facts are ruinous to society, to
decency and to the State's financial fortunes.
Even Texas securities, falling half a per
cent jer diem, may be affected by that con
dition ef society which begets speedy and
violent popular vengeance for crimes com
mitted. "-When mobs supplant courts and
sheriffs, society is practically dissolved and
anarchy inaugurated. To this pitiable con
dition Texas may be reduced. Wretched
systems, of local government have hurried
thousands to poverty. Property, in negro
governed districts n eastern gulf States, is
unsalaole and unproductive. AH profits
of industry are absorbed by the country's
rulers and people reduced to want become
plunderers of corral and of stage coaches.
Crime uuder operative codes in Texas aud
under the existing penitentiary system,
goes, practically, unwbipUof justice and
the very name of .Texas, unless a change be
inaugurated, may be scorned by civilization.
Blaine's followers ascribe our .woes to the
"uiurdeirou spirit of the rebellion, n and
tell .our credulous northern fellow

Texas and the South will be well pleased
that Hayes, of Ohio, and Wheeler, of New
York, are the Republican candidates for the
Presidency and Vice Presidency. Tilden's
nomination becomes, because of issues "in
volved in that of Hayes, a foregone conclu
sion, and the South is no longer the shut
tlecock of partisanship to be crushed
between the upper and nether millstone.
Reform and the currency question will be
the issues, and the bloody shirt is con
verted into the white flag of peace, and
the centennial year is plethoric of bless
ings, and not. of woea, for the couutij.
Hayes is well known everywhere as the
Governor of Ohio, twice or thrice elected
but Wheeler is the greater man of the two.
He is an old and able and wiBe member of
Congress, rendered especially famous by
statecraft illustrated in adjusting troubles
in Louisiana. Both Hayes and Wheeler are
moderate and just, and the South is content.
If Tilden be President, the Union will be
reproduced even as it was; if Hayes, as
perverted by the reign of Radicalism, but
we will have peace and order and good
will and harmony more slowly but surely
ra-establitbed.

Tee proposition made in Congress to
name New Mexico in honor of Montezuma
is hardly rational. The memory of Mon
tezuma is properly despised. He was killed
by a mob of his own countrymen, and was
a weak, despicable and irresolute ruler,
with more wives than Brigham Young or
the late Sultan of Turkey. There is not a
hamlet in Mexico so mean that the people
have named it Montezuma. If Congress
would baptize the new State "Guatemozin,"
the memory of the deeds and 'death of a
hero will be perpetuated;. but let us pot do
violence to the incurable prejudices of a
neighboring Republic by honoring a man
despised everywhere, save by readers of
books, written, as we suppose, to make
Spaniards and their church odious. The
hulls of Montezuma," and not the man,
won the admiration and' excited the
greed of Americans,' and Congress should
not blunder in baptizing, as it has been
criminal in destroying States.
The character aud habits of life of the
great mass of the people of Texas will be
illustrated in the extraordinary crops gar
nered. The very devotion of the great
body of the people to industrial pursuits
is shown in the viofeuce with which they
are disposed to avenge the commission of
crime. They hate robbers and thieves and
worthless vagrants that drift into prosper
ous towns and counties, and therefore the
laws needed to-day, to extirpate villainy,
will spuedily cure evils which the people
deplore, and the harvests of the year giving
us plenty, and reformed codes and juries
and courts giving us order and peace, the
commonwealth may prosper as never, be
fore.

was sought to .appropriate, bis. virtues by

senting their own party m'nothingi. How
ever virtuous the men now nominated, their
party must wear the Nessus's shirt of cor
ruption, of which it is not divested by tear
ing the "bloody shirt" from its reeking,
disgusting body. Tilden has driven tbenv
to mvquerading.

Lee, at the battle of the Wilderness, U pro
nounced to be true -ro life.rto truth and to
history. The great Captain is represented
in a moment of extreme peril with his no
ble countenance lighted by the excitement
of' battle, surrouuded by his admiring and
taithiul soldiers, who are in the act of
making- a charge upon the Federal lines.
and wt'o are made painfully conscious of
the presence of, their: beloved chieltain.
and are crying "Gen. Lee to the rear." In
their intense desire to shield lit in from
harm, they forget aught else, seiz; his bri
dle and turn him back. The groupings are
historically correct. The faces of many .of
the actors are delineated with fiJelity, and
the incidents are not only appropriate, but
are truthfully sketched. Taken as a whole,
the painting is a magnificent work of art: it
is a reproduction on canvas of the terrific
battle scene the final iff ;rt which gave
victory to the Confederates at the Wilder
nes; and it portrays as t wer, in letters
of living light, ,the love of the soldiers for
Uen. Lee. v,
Nor are these the only merits of the
great painting. The faces show conclu
sively that Gen. Lee was at that time sur
rounded by Texas i-troops, and not by
others, as has been incorrectly stated by
nigu authority, in proof, we beg leave to
make an extract from a letter to Mr. Mc
Ardle from Gen. W. N. Pendleton, who
had chief command, .of the artillery:
"It was at this crisis tliat the Texas brig
ade, heading Longstreet's column, began
to emerge from the rear forest along the
plank road,: on the right' portion Of the old
field. Gen. Lee, with grand earnestness.
took off his cap and, waving it, hurrahed
for the Texas brigade, and a general shout
went np as they rapidly formed 'into line.
The General then, giving the word 'charge,'
and sparring his spirited 'Traveler,' pressed
on to lead the charge. This wna observed
with great anxiety by all. The fire waa
furious, the front was scarcely practicable
for men on foot a horseman attempting,
it seemed, going to certain death. Noth
ing but the apparent extremity of the crisis
would have justified Geo. Lee in so inspir
ing the troops. But they needed not this.
Those brave Texans thought less of their
own exposure than of their lovd command
er's. A cry roan; Gen. Lee to the rearl'
He, not heeding, was somewhat roughly
stopped .by a soldier stepping out and seiz
ing 4 Traveler' by the bridle and saying,'
'General, go taxk; if you do not, we will
not go forward.'
The evidence of CoL Venable. Gen. Lee's
aid, is to the same t fleet.
In this view of the case, the Dai mini?
should by all means be the property pf the
Bute of Texas.
The painting of Gen. Stephen F. Austin
at San Felipe, surrounded by Opt. CUna-
man, u iron de Bastrop, Col. Bam W illiams,
Rau Foster and others, is true to life and
to history, and is a masterpiece.
The two are offered at leas tbsn one-third
their value, and your sub-committee tarnest
ly recommend that tbey be purchased by
the State and preserved as invaluable con
tributions to huatory, and models of art.

This king of the orchestra was bora in
1770, at Bonn, on the Rhine. Hi lather
Was a U-norsinger in the Elector of Coloirne'a
ellapel.! Of Beethoven's childhood very lit-
tie is Known, save that) it was passed in most
miserable poverty., He early etinoed, a lik
ing of music, aud bis father, a very dissi
pated man, hearing of the great success of
Mozart as an infant prodigyconceived the
idea of making the like of Lad wig. But the
pian pursued was Jar more, likely to deter
the child from learning anything than from
acquiring a love for music; for often, when
his father, at a lata hour, would return from
the beer houses, the poor boy was forced to
get up and practice, till daybreak. But,
luckily, the child was transferred to anottisr
teacher, and from that time ou made rapid
progress. At tba sge of eleven years he
played all of Bach's preludes and figures (or
pianos,' besides many other works.
In the spring of 1787 youog'Beethovea
started on a viait to the great art capital,
Vienna, where Moztrt and other great ar
tista were living, the chief object his visit
being to obtain aa interview with Mozart.
This was soon accomplished, and Ludwig
waa requested to plav. before the tlren great
idol of the musical world. A theme-was
laid before hi in, on which he wss request, d
to improvise, with what result we all know.
Mozart was struck with it. aiid stood watch
ing with speechless wonder every raTtnrnt
oitneiaa; till,, at last, while, the genius
was winding up amid a' labyrinth, ot melo
dies, Mozart crept stealthily to, another
room, where both critics sad friends had
previously assembled,:, and; with bt face
full of wonder and excitement, exclaimed,
"Take care of this youth some day ha will
make a great stir in the.world."
The aflliction" which early came upon -Beethoven
is thus described in Bit own
words:
Thus with a passionate, lively tempera
ment, keenly susceptible to the channs of
society, I was -forced early to separate my
self from men and lead a solitary life. If at
times I sought to break from my solitude,
how harshly waa I repulaed by tha renewed
consciousness of my afflxtUin, and yet it
was impossible for me to say to feoplt:
'Speak loader, shout I an -deaf." Nor
could I proclaim an imperfection ia that
organ which in me should bsve been more
perfect than in others. What humiliation,
when some one near me bears the note of a
far-off flute and I do not, or the distant
shepherd's song and I do not.
By this terrible affliction, so mncb worse
than bllnduesA, he was obliged,' finally, to
give np piano playing and conducting an
orchestra, and settle, down to corn position
for the rest of bis life. To this fact of his
deafness many theoretic! writers on atusic
.declare we owe the matchless superiority Af
many of Beethovac's later works, for they
assert be caught the soul of music, while
not annoyed by the imperfection of sound.
Though Beethoven was by no means 'so
voluminous a eotnpoawr at- even Mozart,
till his works are much more finished than
those of, any of hi predciora except
Handel and Bath. His life was simple and
uneventful. He was never married, end r.
never even known to hare a love kffaic
As aa instance of the esteem In which he
was neld by the people of Vienna,' mora
than 23,000 of the citizen followed bis re
mains to the grave, where a u tuple stone
mark the but renting place of the greatest
musician of the prest&t century.

INTROSPECTION.
We are becoming, what so many ap
pear to be in our day, practical ma
terialist and atheists. Present life and
surroundings, present profit and pleas
ure are our all in alL We rarely reong
nue the existence of a soul within us
having distinct needs and immortal in
terest. Our thoughts are centered
wholly on tha uotn'orts and pleasure
of the hour and onjliat which advances,
best our earthly ambition. Our skcptl
cism is too coldly intellectual respect
ing the Bible and its teachings, too
critical an i protectant to v ard a Christ
white yet intensely practical in regard to
humanity itself. We believe that with
rare exceptions men aud women live
for their own profit and pleasure, aud
that religion and creeds aud diverse be
liefs, are simply matters of custom and
fashion, or the aecidonts of birth. Only
the recollection of the reverence in
which religion was held in our old
home by onr pious progenitors, prevents
us from sharing fully in the cynicism
and contempt which much of the Wiirld
now entertains for it. We cannot alto
gether dispise our mother' faith, but we
regard her as a gentle enthusiast in
what she does not lully understand.
For preachers vi e have recollections
of passionate and iiltimed sermons or
feelings of distrust based npon our
seeming discovery of afiectation and
hypocricy. Of our future and of Deity,
we have views so vaue, un?ertain and
unsatisfactory that tiie mind refuse to
entertain theut even in its moments of
introspection, and we relajise into the
accustomed groove of business or pleas
ure. Poour Sunday thoughts over
draw the picture, friend?
